serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml glass bottle of Bei Bingyang orange soda, as typically served at Chinese noodle restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Carbonated soft drink, orange flavour — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Fanta Orange, used as closest compositional analogue for sugar-sweetened orange carbonated soda)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bei Bingyang is a Chinese branded orange carbonated soda not listed in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for orange-flavoured carbonated soft drinks (SR Legacy), which are compositionally analogous: water, sugar, carbon dioxide, citric acid, and flavouring. Per-100g values: energy ~42 kcal, carbohydrate ~10.5g (predominantly sugar), protein 0g, fat 0g, sodium ~10mg. These values are consistent with the product's known formulation as a sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage.

gi_source.title: Fanta, orange, carbonated soft drink — GI value 68 (glucose reference); used as analogous estimate for Bei Bingyang orange soda
gi_source.source_match_notes: Bei Bingyang orange soda is not listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is taken from the database entry for Fanta orange carbonated soft drink (glucose scale), which is compositionally analogous (sugar-sweetened, orange-flavoured carbonated beverage). Glycemic load per 330ml serving = (68 × 34.65g carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.6, rounded to 23.8 using 10.5g per 100g × 3.3 = 34.65g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~10 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Bei Bingyang orange soda contains minimal sodium (~33 mg per 330 ml serving), posing negligible direct risk for hypertension; however, high sugar intake from regular consumption may contribute to weight gain and indirectly elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (~34.7g per 330 ml serving) with medium-high glycemic index (GI 68)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Bei Bingyang orange soda, as each 330 ml bottle delivers approximately 34.7g of rapidly absorbed free sugars with a glycemic load of ~23.8, causing significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content linked to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages such as Bei Bingyang orange soda is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with existing CVD should limit intake to occasional consumption only.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bei Bingyang (北冰洋, literally 'Arctic Ocean') is a classic Chinese carbonated orange-flavoured soft drink produced in Beijing, China, since 1951. It is one of the most iconic Chinese soda brands, widely recognised by its distinctive glass bottle and orange-and-white label featuring a polar bear. The beverage is a sugar-sweetened carbonated drink made from water, sugar, carbon dioxide, orange flavouring, citric acid, and preservatives. It has a sweet, lightly tangy orange flavour with moderate carbonation and a refreshing, clean finish. In Singapore, Bei Bingyang is served at Chinese noodle restaurants, particularly those specialising in Lanzhou-style beef noodles, where it is a popular accompaniment. Nutritionally, each 330 ml bottle provides approximately 139 kcal, derived almost entirely from ~34.7g of free sugars (sucrose), with negligible protein, fat, and sodium. It contains no dietary fibre or significant micronutrients. Due to its high free sugar content and medium glycemic index (GI ~68), it poses a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally by those with cardiovascular disease risk. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets.

confidence_reason: Bei Bingyang orange soda is not listed in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for compositionally analogous orange-flavoured carbonated soft drinks. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Fanta orange soda, which is the closest approved analogous food. Confidence is medium because the food is well-characterised as a sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage and the analogue is compositionally very close, but no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific brand.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: Bei Bingyang orange soda is a sugar-water carbonated beverage containing no protein by formulation. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous orange carbonated soft drink data. 2. fat_g is naturally zero: this product contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous data. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero as the product contains no fat. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre by formulation. 5. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero: no animal-derived ingredients are present. 6. potassium_mg is -1: not reported in the USDA FoodData Central analogous entry used for estimation. 7. calcium_mg is -1: not reported in the source analogue. 8. iron_mg is -1: not reported in the source analogue. 9. vitamin_c_mg is -1: orange flavouring does not guarantee ascorbic acid content; not reported in source analogue. 10. vitamin_a_mcg_rae through selenium_mcg are -1: micronutrient data not reported in the USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for orange carbonated soft drinks. 11. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (Fanta orange soda); Bei Bingyang is not directly listed. 12. The food_group has been corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Beverages', consistent with the product's nature as a carbonated soft drink. 13. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Chinese cuisine served in Singapore context.
